researchFindings.claim: You will likely not be able to tell the difference between a sprain, dislocation, and fracture without an X-ray. Treat all suspected bone/joint injuries as potential fractures and decide whether to call 911 based on signs and symptoms.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Muscle, Bone and Joint Injury — First Aid

researchFindings.claim: Signs that suggest a fracture: severe pain that worsens with movement or pressure, rapid swelling and bruising, deformity (limb bent at unnatural angle, shortened, or rotated), inability to bear weight or move the area.

Call 911 for: compound/open fractures (bone through skin), large bone fractures (femur/hip/pelvis), signs of shock, suspected head/neck/spinal injury, loss of sensation or circulation below the injury.
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: When to call 911 — redFlags and warnings

researchFindings.claim: Splint an injury in the position you find it. Never try to straighten or realign a deformed limb or push a bone protruding through the skin back in.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Muscle, Bone and Joint Injury — First Aid
researchFindings.fieldRelevance: Core immobilization rule — stepByStepActions and whatNotToDo

researchFindings.claim: When splinting, immobilize the joint above AND below the injury. For example, a forearm fracture requires the splint to extend from above the elbow to below the wrist.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Muscle, Bone and Joint Injury — First Aid

researchFindings.claim: Improvised splints can be made from rigid materials: rolled magazine, piece of wood, umbrella, pillow (for ankle/foot), or bind an injured leg to the uninjured leg using triangular bandages. Pad the splint with soft material to prevent pressure injuries.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Muscle, Bone and Joint Injury — First Aid

researchFindings.claim: Check for circulation (feeling, warmth, skin color) below the injury site both before and after applying a splint. Recheck regularly. Numbness, coldness, or pale/blue skin below the injury are emergency signs.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Muscle, Bone and Joint Injury — First Aid

researchFindings.claim: Apply a cold pack wrapped in cloth to the injured area for up to 20 minutes at a time to reduce swelling and pain. Ice or cooling may be applied for up to 20 consecutive minutes to sprained joints and soft-tissue injuries. Do not apply ice directly to skin or for longer than 20 consecutive minutes — this can damage the skin.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Muscle, Bone and Joint Injury — First Aid

researchFindings.claim: Elevate the injured area above heart level if possible and if it does not cause more pain. This reduces swelling and promotes drainage of excess fluid.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Muscle, Bone and Joint Injury — First Aid

researchFindings.claim: All fractures should be assessed for internal and external bleeding. Treat for shock if the person is pale, cold, faint, or breathing rapidly — especially for large-bone fractures (femur can lose 1–2 liters of blood internally).
Do not ask the person to move or test the injured area. Do not use tight compression directly over a suspected fracture or open wound.
researchFindings.sourceTitle: Muscle, Bone and Joint Injury — First Aid

researchFindings.claim: For suspected head, neck, or spinal injuries: tell the person not to move, leave them in position found, and call 911. Do not move them unless needed for safety, CPR, or to control bleeding.

conflictsOrWeakClaims.claim: Use POLICE (Protection, Optimal Loading, Ice, Compression, Elevation) rather than RICE
conflictsOrWeakClaims.issue: POLICE is the preferred modern sports medicine framework for ongoing rehabilitation. However, Red Cross 2024 first aid guidelines use RICE (Rest, Immobilize, Cool, Elevate) for the acute bystander phase — which is what this action card covers. POLICE is relevant for recovery, not the emergency first 30 minutes.
conflictsOrWeakClaims.sourceA: Red Cross / AHA 2024 First Aid Guidelines (RICE for acute phase)
conflictsOrWeakClaims.resolution: Use RICE framing (Rest, Immobilize, Cool, Elevate) for the action card. Note that a medical professional will guide ongoing care. Do not include POLICE guidance — it is beyond the bystander scope of this guide.

identifiedGaps.impact: Compression is part of RICE/PRICE but Red Cross 2024 explicitly warns against tight compression directly over suspected fractures. For sprains only, compression is appropriate. Including it in a guide that covers both would risk misapplication.
identifiedGaps.suggestion: Omit compression from steps. Note in whatNotToDo that tight compression should not be applied over a suspected fracture or open wound. Defer compression technique to the follow-up scenario guide.

recommendation: Write a new action_card for the first ~30 minutes of a sprain or suspected fracture. Scope: bystander response, limb injuries (not spine/skull). Primary action: RICE — stop movement, improvised splint, cool (wrapped ice 20 min), elevate. Emphasize 'treat as fracture if unsure' and the circulation check. Call 911 criteria clearly listed as red flags. Sources: Red Cross Muscle/Bone/Joint and Fractures pages — sourceQuality: strong.
